content.text: Strings are the primary data type for all text in Python — prompts, API responses, user messages, file paths, log entries, and configuration values. Choosing the right formatting method and understanding string internals prevents performance bugs and subtle encoding issues at scale.
A string in Python 3 is an immutable sequence of Unicode code points (

type). 'Immutable' means no string method modifies the string in place — all operations return new string objects. The original string is never changed, which makes strings safe to share across threads and safe to use as dictionary keys.
Python 3 uses Unicode for all string data by default: every
can contain any Unicode character. The separate
type represents raw binary data. Encoding a string (
) produces a string. Confusing these two types is the most common source of
TypeError: a bytes-like object is required
Python provides four string formatting approaches: f-strings (
(safe for user-supplied templates). Each has different performance, readability, and security characteristics.

In agentic AI systems, string operations appear everywhere: constructing LLM prompts, parsing tool call arguments, formatting JSON payloads, building log messages, validating user input, and processing API responses. A misunderstood string operation — using
in a loop for prompt building, failing to strip whitespace from parsed output, or mixing bytes and str — will not raise an exception until it hits a code path in production.
f-strings were introduced specifically because the previous approaches were either too slow (
formatting with tuples), or too verbose. Understanding why each method exists helps in choosing the right one for the right context and in reading existing code that uses the older methods.
String performance also matters at scale: building a 10,000-token prompt from 500 individual parts using
is an O(n^2) operation that can take hundreds of milliseconds. Using
completes in microseconds. Knowing this difference matters when constructing prompts dynamically in an agentic pipeline.
f-strings are evaluated at runtime with full Python expression support inside the braces — arithmetic, method calls, conditional expressions, and attribute access all work.
Format specification mini-language provides precise control over number formatting (precision, width, sign, thousands separator) and string alignment without requiring manual slicing.
String immutability makes strings safe as dict keys, set members, and shared objects across threads — no defensive copying is needed.
Python strings support a rich set of methods (
) that cover the vast majority of text processing needs without importing anything.
) preserve newlines and are ideal for multi-line prompts, SQL queries, and docstrings.
) disable backslash escape processing, making regex patterns and Windows paths readable without escaping every backslash.
) provides powerful substring extraction using the same semantics as list slicing.
String immutability means building a string by concatenation in a loop is O(n^2) — each
allocates a new string. Always use
f-strings are Python 3.6+ and cannot be used in code that must run on Python 3.5 or older (rare today but relevant for embedded systems).
f-strings evaluate the expression eagerly at the point of construction — they cannot be stored as templates and filled in later. Use
) requires the right-hand side to be a tuple when more than one value is provided — missing the tuple is a silent bug that formats incorrectly.
Unicode handling requires explicit encoding/decoding at I/O boundaries. Reading a file with the wrong encoding silently produces wrong results or raises
Python string literals can be prefixed with:
for raw bytes. Prefixes can be combined:
Escape sequences inside regular string literals:
(Unicode code point). Raw strings suppress all of these —
is a two-character string containing backslash and 'n'.
f-strings (formatted string literals) are evaluated at the point where they appear in the source code. The content inside

is a full Python expression: arithmetic, method calls, attribute access, ternary expressions, and even lambda functions (with parentheses) are all valid. A colon after the expression introduces a format specification:
is now valid. Python 3.8 added the
, which is very useful in print-debugging.
The format specification (the part after the colon in
processes a format string where placeholders are identified by

. It is more powerful than f-strings in one way: the format string can be stored in a variable and applied later, enabling template patterns. It also supports attribute access and index access inside placeholders:
At I/O boundaries — files, network sockets, subprocess output — data arrives as bytes. Always specify the encoding explicitly when converting:
shortcuts that rely on platform-default encoding — the default varies between Linux (usually UTF-8), Windows (often CP1252), and macOS.
The diagram shows Python's four string formatting methods and key string properties.

attrs.alt: Python string formatting methods: f-strings, str.format(), percent formatting, Template

content.text: : Default choice for new code. Best when the values are available at the point of construction. Readable, fast, and supports full Python expressions.
: Use when the template is stored separately from the values — e.g., a prompt template loaded from a config file.
) because Python's logging module defers % formatting until the message is actually emitted, saving computation when the log level is disabled.
: Use when the template comes from user input or an untrusted source.
leaves missing keys as-is rather than raising an error, preventing injection via format placeholders.
in any loop that accumulates string pieces. This is an O(n) operation vs O(n^2) for repeated concatenation.

# --- String literals and escape sequences ---
regular = "Hello\nWorld"        # newline in string
raw = r"Hello\nWorld"           # literal backslash + n
triple = """Line 1
Line 2
Line 3"""                          # preserves newlines

# --- f-strings ---
name = "Alice"
score = 98.6
rank = 1
print(f"Player: {name}, Score: {score:.1f}, Rank: {rank:03d}")
# Player: Alice, Score: 98.6, Rank: 001

# Expressions inside f-strings
items = ["a", "b", "c"]
print(f"Count: {len(items)}, Upper: {', '.join(x.upper() for x in items)}")
# Count: 3, Upper: A, B, C

# Debug specifier (Python 3.8+)
x = 42
print(f"{x=}")           # x=42

# --- str.format() ---
template = "Dear {name}, your order #{order_id} ships on {date}."
msg = template.format(name="Bob", order_id=1234, date="2024-01-15")
print(msg)

# Positional
print("{0} + {1} = {2}".format(3, 4, 7))

# --- Percent formatting (common in logging) ---
import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
logging.info("Processing %d items for user %s", 42, "alice")

# --- string.Template ---
from string import Template
t = Template("Hello, $name! Your balance is $$${amount:.2f}")
# NOTE: $$ produces literal $; then {amount:.2f} is plain text inside {}
t2 = Template("Hello, $name!")
print(t2.safe_substitute(name="Carol"))      # Hello, Carol!
print(t2.safe_substitute())                  # Hello, $name!  (safe: no error)

# --- Efficient concatenation ---
parts = ["Part " + str(i) for i in range(5)]
# WRONG: O(n^2)
result = ""
for part in parts:
    result += part + " "
print(result.strip())

# CORRECT: O(n)
result = " ".join(parts)
print(result)

# Number formatting
pi = 3.141592653589793
print(f"{pi:.2f}")         # 3.14  (2 decimal places)
print(f"{pi:10.3f}")       # (width=10, 3 decimal places, right-aligned)
print(f"{pi:e}")           # 3.141593e+00  (scientific)
print(f"{1234567.89:,.2f}") # 1,234,567.89 (thousands separator)
print(f"{0.004567:.4%}")   # 0.4567%  (percent)

# Integer formatting
n = 255
print(f"{n:d}")    # 255   (decimal)
print(f"{n:b}")    # 11111111  (binary)
print(f"{n:o}")    # 377   (octal)
print(f"{n:x}")    # ff    (hex lowercase)
print(f"{n:X}")    # FF    (hex uppercase)
print(f"{n:#x}")   # 0xff  (with prefix)
print(f"{n:08b}")  # 11111111  (zero-padded binary, width 8)

# String alignment
header = "Name"
print(f"{header:<10}")   # "Name      "  (left)
print(f"{header:>10}")   # "      Name"  (right)
print(f"{header:^10}")   # "   Name   "  (center)
print(f"{header:-^10}")  # "---Name---"  (fill character)

# Practical: nicely formatted table
headers = ["Name", "Score", "Grade"]
rows = [("Alice", 92.5, "A"), ("Bob", 78.3, "C+"), ("Carol", 88.0, "B+")]
print(f"{'Name':<10} {'Score':>6} {'Grade':>5}")
print("-" * 24)
for name, score, grade in rows:
    print(f"{name:<10} {score:>6.1f} {grade:>5}")

s = "  Hello, World!  "

# Stripping whitespace
print(repr(s.strip()))           # "Hello, World!"
print(repr(s.lstrip()))          # "Hello, World!  "
print(repr(s.rstrip()))          # "  Hello, World!"

# Case methods
print(s.strip().upper())         # "HELLO, WORLD!"
print(s.strip().lower())         # "hello, world!"
print(s.strip().title())         # "Hello, World!"
print(s.strip().swapcase())      # "hELLO, wORLD!"

# Searching
text = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog"
print(text.find("fox"))          # 16 (index of first occurrence)
print(text.find("cat"))          # -1 (not found)
print(text.index("fox"))         # 16 (raises ValueError if not found)
print(text.count("the"))         # 1 (case-sensitive)
print(text.count("e"))           # 3
print(text.startswith("The"))    # True
print(text.endswith("dog"))      # True

# Splitting and joining
csv = "apple,banana,cherry"
parts = csv.split(",")           # ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
print(parts)
print("|".join(parts))           # "apple|banana|cherry"

lines = "line1\nline2\nline3".splitlines()
print(lines)                     # ['line1', 'line2', 'line3']

# Replacing
print(text.replace("fox", "cat"))  # "The quick brown cat jumps..."
print(text.replace("the", "a", 1)) # replace only first occurrence

# Checking content
print("42".isdigit())            # True
print("hello".isalpha())         # True
print("hello world".isalpha())   # False (space)
print("".isdigit())              # False (empty)

# Encoding/decoding
encoded = "Héllo".encode("utf-8")
print(encoded)                   # b'H\xc3\xa9llo'
print(encoded.decode("utf-8"))   # "Héllo"
Example 3: Multi-line Prompts and Template Building

# Building LLM prompts — practical agentic patterns

# Multi-line prompt with triple quotes
system_prompt = """You are a helpful assistant for analyzing Python code.
Your responses should be precise and include code examples.
Always use f-strings for string formatting in your code examples."""

# Template stored separately from values
TOOL_RESULT_TEMPLATE = """Tool: {tool_name}
Status: {status}
Result:
{result}
---
"""

def format_tool_result(tool_name: str, status: str, result: str) -> str:
    return TOOL_RESULT_TEMPLATE.format(
        tool_name=tool_name,
        status=status,
        result=result
    )

# Efficient dynamic prompt construction with join
def build_prompt(system: str, history: list[dict], user_msg: str) -> str:
    """Build a chat prompt from system message, history, and new user message."""
    parts = [f"System: {system}", ""]
    for turn in history:
        role = turn["role"].capitalize()
        parts.append(f"{role}: {turn['content']}")
    parts.append(f"User: {user_msg}")
    parts.append("Assistant:")
    return "\n".join(parts)   # O(n) join, not O(n^2) concatenation

history = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "What is Python?"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "Python is a high-level language."},
]
prompt = build_prompt(system_prompt, history, "How do I format numbers?")
print(prompt[:200])

In a production LLM pipeline, structured outputs from models are typically JSON strings that must be parsed, validated, and re-formatted for display or downstream use. String manipulation appears at every stage.

"""
Production string handling in an LLM API response pipeline.
"""
import json
import re
from typing import Optional

def extract_json_from_llm_output(raw: str) -> Optional[dict]:
    """
    LLM outputs often wrap JSON in markdown code blocks.
    Extract and parse the JSON content.
    """
    # Pattern 1: JSON in markdown code block
    md_pattern = r"```(?:json)?\s*\n?([\s\S]*?)\n?```"
    match = re.search(md_pattern, raw.strip())
    if match:
        candidate = match.group(1).strip()
    else:
        # Pattern 2: raw JSON starting with { or [
        candidate = raw.strip()
        if not (candidate.startswith("{") or candidate.startswith("[")):
            return None

    try:
        return json.loads(candidate)
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        return None


def format_citation(title: str, url: str, snippet: str,
                    max_snippet_len: int = 200) -> str:
    """Format a search result citation for inclusion in a prompt."""
    # Truncate snippet cleanly at word boundary
    if len(snippet) > max_snippet_len:
        snippet = snippet[:max_snippet_len].rsplit(" ", 1)[0] + "..."
    # Clean up whitespace
    title = " ".join(title.split())
    snippet = " ".join(snippet.split())
    return f"[{title}]({url})\n> {snippet}"


def sanitize_user_input(text: str, max_length: int = 4096) -> str:
    """Sanitize user input before embedding in a prompt."""
    # Normalize whitespace
    text = " ".join(text.split())
    # Truncate if too long
    if len(text) > max_length:
        text = text[:max_length].rsplit(" ", 1)[0] + " [truncated]"
    return text.strip()


# Test
raw_llm = """Sure! Here's the data you requested:
```json
{"action": "search", "query": "python tutorials", "limit": 5}
```
"""
result = extract_json_from_llm_output(raw_llm)
print(result)  # {'action': 'search', 'query': 'python tutorials', 'limit': 5}

citation = format_citation(
    "Python Documentation",
    "https://docs.python.org",
    "Python is a programming language that lets you work quickly " * 10,
    max_snippet_len=80
)
print(citation)

clean = sanitize_user_input("  Hello   world\n\nHow  are   you?  ")
print(repr(clean))  # 'Hello world How are you?'

# WRONG: O(n^2) — creates a new string object on every +=
parts = [f"item_{i}" for i in range(10000)]
result = ""
for part in parts:
    result += part + ","   # allocates new string each time
# For 10,000 items: ~10^8 character copies total

# CORRECT: O(n) — one allocation for the final string
result = ",".join(parts)  # build all at once

# CORRECT alternative: list append then join
chunks = []
for part in parts:
    chunks.append(part)
result = ",".join(chunks)

# For prompt building specifically
prompt_parts = []
prompt_parts.append("System: Be helpful.")
for msg in messages:
    prompt_parts.append(f"{msg['role']}: {msg['content']}")
prompt = "\n".join(prompt_parts)

# WRONG: relies on platform-default encoding
with open("data.txt") as f:
    content = f.read()  # encoding varies by platform (Windows: CP1252, Unix: UTF-8)

# CORRECT: always specify encoding explicitly
with open("data.txt", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    content = f.read()

# WRONG: mixing bytes and str
data = b"Hello"
# result = data + " World"  # TypeError: can't concat str to bytes

# CORRECT: decode first or encode the str
result = data + b" World"     # bytes + bytes
result = data.decode("utf-8") + " World"   # str + str

# WRONG: single value without tuple — looks fine for str, fails for tuples
value = "hello"
print("Value: %s" % value)   # OK

# WRONG: passing a tuple as the single value
coordinates = (3, 4)
try:
    print("Point: %s" % coordinates)  # TypeError: not enough arguments for format string
except TypeError as e:
    print(e)

# CORRECT: wrap in an outer tuple
print("Point: %s" % (coordinates,))   # Point: (3, 4)

# BETTER: use f-string or .format() — no tuple pitfall
print(f"Point: {coordinates}")         # Point: (3, 4)
print("Point: {}".format(coordinates)) # Point: (3, 4)
Use f-strings for all new formatting code unless you need a separately stored template (use
for building strings from multiple pieces in a loop. Never use
in a loop for string construction.
explicitly when opening files for reading or writing text.
) for regex patterns to avoid double-escaping backslashes.
Use triple-quoted strings for multi-line prompts and docstrings. They are cleaner than escaped
) before comparison, storage, or embedding in prompts.
) rather than f-strings — the formatting is deferred until the message is actually logged, saving computation when the log level is disabled.

CPython performs some string interning for string literals that look like identifiers (short strings of letters, digits, and underscores). This means the same literal in different files may share a single object. Do not rely on this: always compare strings with

For bulk text processing (processing megabytes of log data, building million-token prompts), the choice of string manipulation method dominates performance. The fastest approach for string building is
on a pre-built list. The second fastest is using
on it. The slowest is repeated
removes common leading whitespace from triple-quoted strings;
wraps text to a fixed width.
: Regular expressions for pattern matching and substitution. Use
to pre-compile patterns used in loops.
: In-memory string buffer supporting file-like interface — useful for incremental string building without repeated concatenation.

# jinja2: full template engine for HTML/text generation
# pip install jinja2
from jinja2 import Template
t = Template("Hello, {{ name }}! You have {{ count }} messages.")
print(t.render(name="Alice", count=3))

# rapidfuzz: fast fuzzy string matching
# pip install rapidfuzz
from rapidfuzz import fuzz
print(fuzz.ratio("hello world", "hello wrold"))  # similarity score

# ftfy: fixes broken Unicode text (mojibake, etc.)
# pip install ftfy
import ftfy
print(ftfy.fix_text("Ã©l\x00ve"))  # fixes common encoding errors
Passing user-controlled strings as format templates is a server-side template injection vulnerability. An attacker who controls the format string in
or f-string source can call arbitrary Python attributes (
) and potentially escalate to code execution. Always use
when the template source is user-controlled.

# DANGEROUS: user controls format string
user_template = "{obj.__class__.__bases__}"  # can access internals
class Config:
    SECRET = "hunter2"
config = Config()
# print(user_template.format(obj=config))  # exposes class hierarchy

# SAFE: use string.Template — no attribute access syntax
from string import Template
user_template = Template("Hello, $name!")
print(user_template.safe_substitute(name="Alice"))  # "Hello, Alice!"
# Attribute access not possible through Template
Reading a file with the wrong encoding produces garbled text (mojibake) or raises
to handle files that may contain invalid byte sequences for the given encoding.
Building file paths by concatenating user input with
or f-strings can produce path traversal vulnerabilities (
and validate that the resolved path is within the expected directory:

Think of a Python string as a glass paperweight: beautiful, useful, and completely solid. You cannot add to it, remove from it, or change any part of it once it is made. Every operation that appears to modify a string —
, slicing, concatenation — actually casts a new paperweight with the desired properties.
f-strings work like a mail merge: you write a document with blank fields (the expressions inside
), fill in the values at the moment of use, and get a complete document. The template and the values are combined exactly once. For stored templates, switch to
— the equivalent of having the document pre-printed and filling in values later with different ink.
Python 3 strings are Unicode sequences of code points, immutable by design. Encoding to bytes and decoding from bytes must be explicit, always with a specified encoding (prefer UTF-8).
) for all new string formatting code. They are the most readable, fastest, and most capable method available in Python 3.6+.
in any loop that builds a string. Repeated concatenation is O(n^2); join is O(n).
f-strings evaluate expressions immediately. For templates that must be stored and filled in later, use
) for regex patterns and Windows file paths to avoid needing to double-escape backslashes.
when opening files. Never rely on the platform default encoding.
The logging module should use percent formatting (
) because it defers string formatting until the message is actually emitted.
Never pass user-controlled strings as format templates to
which has no attribute access syntax.
